zh.Ulcombe, Kent, England: 英國英格蘭肯特武爾可姆
zh.lifeSummary: 當William de St Leger於1145年出生在英國英格蘭肯特武爾可姆時，他的父親Reginald de St Leger31歲，母親Helisende d'Eu25歲。他於1179年與Joan de Sackville在英國英格蘭艾可結婚。他們至少是3個兒子和1個女兒的父母。他去世於1220年享年75歲。

zh-hans.Ulcombe, Kent, England: 英国英格兰肯特武爾可姆
zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当William de St Leger于1145年出生在英国英格兰肯特武爾可姆时，他的父亲Reginald de St Leger31岁，母亲Helisende d'Eu25岁。他于1179年与Joan de Sackville在英国英格兰艾可结婚。他们至少是3个儿子和1个女儿的父母。他去世于1220年享年75岁。

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: St
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: 1  English (of Norman origin): habitational name from any of the numerous places in France so called from the dedication of their churches to St. Jean (  see  John  ).
2  Americanized form of French   St  .   Jean  .

variants.name: John
meanings: English and Welsh: ultimately from the Hebrew personal name Yoḥanan ‘Jehovah has favored (me with a son)’ or ‘may Jehovah favor (this child)’. This personal name was adopted into Latin (via Greek) as Johannes, and has enjoyed enormous popularity in Europe throughout the Christian era, being given in honor of Saint John the Baptist, precursor of Christ, and of Saint John the Evangelist, author of the fourth gospel, as well as the nearly one thousand other Christian saints who bore the name. Some of the principal forms of the personal name in other languages are: Welsh Ieuan, Evan, Siôn, and Ioan; Scottish Ia(i)n; Irish Séan; German Johann, Johannes; Dutch and Slavic Jan; French Jean; Italian Giovanni; Spanish Juan; Portuguese João; Greek Iōannēs (vernacular Giannis, Yannis); Russian Ivan. There were a number of different forms of the name in Middle English, including Jan(e), a male name (see Jayne ); Jen (see Jenkin ); Jon(e) (see Jones ); and Han(n) (see Hann ). By the beginning of the 14th century John rivalled William in popularity and has always been a favorite name. Johan became Jo(h)n, and another Old French form Jehan was shortened to Jan and Jen, giving rise to Old French and Middle English diminutives such as Jonin, Janin, and Jenin. More common in Middle English were Jankin, Jonkin, and Jenkin, which were Middle Dutch pet forms introduced after the Conquest by Flemish and Picard settlers. The most common pet form of John was Jack, another borrowing from Flemish and Picard usage. Han may sometimes have been a short form of Johan but was more usually a pet form of Henry. There were also various Middle English feminine versions of this name (e.g. Joan, Jehan), some of which were indistinguishable from masculine forms. The distinction on grounds of gender between John and Joan was not firmly established in English until the 17th century. It was even later that Jean and Jane were specialized as specifically female names in English; bearers of these surnames and their derivatives are more likely to derive them from a male ancestor than a female. As a surname in the British Isles, John is particularly frequent in Wales, where it is a late formation representing Welsh Siôn rather than the older form Ieuan (which gave rise to the surname Evan ). In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed many cognates from other languages, e.g. Assyrian/Chaldean Youkhana , French Jean , Hungarian János (see Janos ), Slovenian Janež and Janeš (see Janes ), Czech Jan , Albanian Gjoni , and their derivatives (see examples at Johnson ). The name John is also found among Christians in southern India (compare Ninan and Yohannan ), but since South Indians traditionally do not have hereditary surnames, the southern Indian name was in most cases registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US.
German: from a North German and Silesian variant of the personal name   Johannes  . This surname is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine). Compare   Yohn  .

variants.name: Jean
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Francois, Marcel, Yves, Andre, Jacques, Georges, Michel, Philippe, Serge, Yvon, Cecile.

French, Walloon, West Indian (mainly Haiti), Mauritian, and English (of French Huguenot origin): from the male personal name Jean, French form of John . This is one of the most frequent surnames in Haiti (along with Joseph and Pierre ). It is also established elsewhere in the West Indies (e.g. in the Dominican Republic, Saint Lucia, Saint Martin, and Bahamas).
English: habitational name for someone from Genoa in Italy; compare   Janeway  .

nameMeanings.name: Leger
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Camille, Lucien, Pierre, Yvon, Antoine, Aurele, Emile, Jacques, Marcel, Zoel, Adelard.

French (Léger) and English: from the ancient Germanic personal name Leudgari, composed of the elements leud ‘people’ and gari (from garwa) ‘ready, eager’ or gari ‘spear’ and Latinized as Leodegar(ius). The name was borne by a 7th-century Christian saint, bishop of Autun, whose fame contributed to the popularity of the name in France. See also Ledger and St. Leger .
French (Léger): nickname from léger ‘light, superficial’.

variants.name: Lege
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Alphie, Emile, Gaston, Henri, Leonce, Minus, Numa, Pierre, Rodolph.
German: topographic name meaning ‘swamp’.
Altered form of French Léger (see Leger ).

variants.name: Legere
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Emile, Armand, Normand, Aurele, Jacques, Adelard, Alban, Antoine, Desire, Eloi, Monique, Yvon.
French (Légère): nickname from légère, a feminine form of Léger ‘light, superficial’ (see Leger 2).
Altered form of French   Leger   1 or 2.

variants.name: Lougee
meanings: Channel Islands: altered form of Laugee, a variant of French Lauger, itself either a variant of Léger (see Leger ) or a variant, with fused definite article l', of Auger .

History: The first recorded American bearer of this surname was John Lougee from the isle of Jersey. Born Jean Laugee and by trade a knitter, he was aged c. 18 or 19 when he emigrated to the USA in the late 1600s, settling in Exeter, NH, where he became known as John Lougee, marrying Anne, the daughter of Colonel Moses Gilman.

variants.name: Ledger
meanings: English: from a Norman personal name, Leodegar, Old French Legier, of ancient Germanic origin (see Leger 1).
English: metonymic occupational name from Middle English legger ‘one who lays stone, a mason’, a derivative of Old English lecgan ‘to lay’.
Americanized form of French   Leger   1.

variants.name: St. Leger
meanings: French (Saint-Léger): habitational name from any of several places in various parts of France named Saint-Léger (from the dedication of their churches to Saint Leodegar, in French St. Léger, the martyred 7th-century bishop of Autun), or a nickname with the prefix Saint as an (ironic) attachment to the personal name or surname Léger (see Leger 1).

Haitian (also Saint-Léger): from Saint-Léger, a nickname, ornamental name, or personal name composed of the French prefix Saint and the personal name Léger (see Leger 1), or perhaps a habitational name from a place in Haiti called Saint-Léger (compare 1 above).
English (of Norman origin): habitational name from Saint-Léger in La Manche or Saint-Léger-aux-Bois in Seine-Maritime, France (see 1 above).

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

en.lifeSummary: When William de St Leger was born in 1145, in Ulcombe, Kent, England, United Kingdom, his father, Reginald de St Leger, was 31 and his mother, Helisende d'Eu, was 25. He married Joan de Sackville in 1179, in Essex, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 3 sons and 1 daughter. He died in 1220, at the age of 75.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque William de St Leger est né en 1145, à Ulcombe, Kent, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, son père, Reginald de St Leger, avait 31 ans et sa mère, Helisende d'Eu, avait 25 ans. Il a épousé Joan de Sackville en 1179, à Essex, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 3 fils et 1 fille. Il est décédé en 1220, à l’âge de 75 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als William de St Leger 1145 in Ulcombe, Kent, England, United Kingdom geboren wurde, war sein Vater Reginald de St Leger 31 und seine Mutter Helisende d'Eu 25. Er hat 1179 in Essex, England, United Kingdom Joan de Sackville geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 3 Söhne und 1 Tochter. Er starb 1220 im Alter von 75 Jahren.

it.lifeSummary: Quando William de St Leger è nato il nel 1145 nel Ulcombe, Kent, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, il suo padre, Reginald de St Leger, aveva 31 anni e il suo madre, Helisende d'Eu, aveva 25 anni. Lui sposato Joan de Sackville nel 1179, nel Essex, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Erano genitori di almeno 3 figli e 1 figlia. Morì nel 1220, all'età di 75 anni.

ko.lifeSummary: William de St Leger(이)가 1145년 영국 잉글랜드 켄트 울코브에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Reginald de St Leger(은)는 31세였고, 그의 어머니 Helisende d'Eu(은)는 25세였다. 그는 1179년 영국 잉글랜드 에세스에서 Joan de Sackville(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 세 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1220년 75세의 나이로 사망했다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда William de St Leger родился в 1145 г. в местности улкомб, кэнт, Англия, Великобритания, его отцу по имени Reginald de St Leger было 31 год, а матери, которую звали Helisende d'Eu, 25. Он женился на женщине по имени Joan de Sackville в 1179 г. в местности эссекс, Англия, Великобритания. У них было по крайней мере 3 сына и 1 дочь. Он умер в 1220 г. в возрасте 75 лет.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando William de St Leger nació en 1145, en Ulcombe, Kent, Inglaterra, Reino Unido, su papá, Reginald de St Leger, tenía 31 años y su mamá, Helisende d'Eu, tenía 25 años. Se casó con Joan de Sackville en 1179, en Essex, Inglaterra, Reino Unido. Fueron padres de por lo menos 3 hijos y 1 hija. Murió en 1220, a la edad de 75 años.
